subtopics.title: Introduction to Statistics – Data, Variables & Scope

blocks.title: What is Statistics?
blocks.content: Statistics is the science of collecting, organizing, analyzing, interpreting, and presenting data. It is crucial for decision-making under uncertainty.

blocks.title: Types of Data
blocks.content:
1. Primary Data: Collected firsthand (e.g., surveys).
2. Secondary Data: Published by others (e.g., government reports).
3. Qualitative (Categorical) vs Quantitative (Numerical).

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Statistics helps extract meaningful insights from raw data.
Understanding data types determines the appropriate analysis method.
Data Interpretation (DI) is a major chunk of the numerical ability section.

subtopics.title: Measures of Central Tendency – Arithmetic Mean

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.content: The arithmetic mean is the sum of all observations divided by the total number of observations.

blocks.title: Mean Formulas
blocks.formula:
Ungrouped: Mean = Σx / n
Grouped: Mean = Σ(f×x) / Σf
blocks.description: For grouped data, 'x' is the class midpoint and 'f' is the frequency.

blocks.title: Trap: Class Midpoints
blocks.wrong: Using class boundaries (e.g., 0, 10) instead of midpoints.
blocks.tip: Always compute class marks accurately for grouped data.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Mean = Sum of observations / Count.
For grouped data, use frequencies and midpoints.
Mean is sensitive to extreme values (outliers).

blocks.title: Median
blocks.subtitle: The Middle Value of a Dataset

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.content: The median is the value that separates the higher half from the lower half of a dataset. It is robust to outliers.

blocks.title: Example 2: Even Number of Observations
steps: n = 6 (even), Median = average of 3rd and 4th terms.
blocks.formula_used: Average of middle two for even n.

blocks.title: Median for Grouped Data
blocks.content:
Median = L + [ (n/2 – cf) / f ] × h
Where L = lower boundary of median class, cf = cumulative frequency before median class, f = frequency of median class, h = class width.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Median is not affected by extreme values.
For odd n: median = (n+1)/2th value.
For even n: median = average of n/2 and (n/2+1)th values.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.content: Mode is the value that appears most frequently. A dataset can be unimodal (one mode), bimodal (two modes), or multimodal.

blocks.title: Example: Find the Mode
steps: 6 appears 3 times, which is the highest frequency.

blocks.title: Mode for Grouped Data
blocks.content:
Mode = L + [ (f₁ – f₀) / (2f₁ – f₀ – f₂) ] × h
Where L = lower boundary of modal class, f₁ = frequency of modal class, f₀ = previous class frequency, f₂ = next class frequency, h = class width.

blocks.title: Trap: Confusing Mode with Mean/Median
blocks.wrong: Calculating average when asked for the most frequent value.
blocks.correct: Mode is strictly based on frequency distribution.
blocks.tip: Check which value/class appears the most.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Mode = most frequent observation.
Can be used for categorical data as well.
For grouped data, use the modal class formula.

subtopics.title: Measures of Dispersion – Range, Variance, Standard Deviation

blocks.title: What is Dispersion?
blocks.content: Dispersion measures how spread out the data values are. Common measures include Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation.

blocks.title: Formulas
blocks.formula:
Range = Max – Min
Variance (σ²) = Σ (xᵢ – μ)² / n
Standard Deviation (σ) = √Variance
blocks.description: Standard deviation is the most widely used measure of spread.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Range = Max – Min.
Variance = average of squared deviations from the mean.
SD = square root of variance.
Larger SD indicates more spread.

blocks.title: What are Tables?
blocks.content: Tables present data in rows and columns. They are the most common format in competitive exams.

blocks.title: Example 1: Total Sales in 2022
blocks.find: Total sales of all companies in 2022.

blocks.title: Example 2: Ratio of Sales
blocks.find: Ratio of sales of A to D in 2021.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Read rows and columns carefully.
Identify what is asked: total, average, ratio, percentage, or difference.
Practice mental arithmetic to save time.

blocks.title: Bar Graphs
blocks.content: Use rectangular bars to compare categories. Bars can be vertical or horizontal.

blocks.title: Line Graphs
blocks.content: Connect data points with lines to show trends over time (e.g., growth, decline).

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Bar graphs compare categories; line graphs show trends.
Check the axes and units carefully.
Calculate totals, averages, and growth rates quickly.

blocks.title: What is a Pie Chart?
blocks.content: A circular chart divided into sectors. The total angle is 360° and total percentage is 100%.

blocks.title: Pie Chart Conversions
blocks.description: Use these to interpret pie chart data.

blocks.title: Example: Find Expenditure
given: Pie chart: Food=30%, Rent=25%, Transport=20%, Others=25%.

blocks.title: Example: Find the Angle
given: Transport sector is 20% of the total.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Value = (Percentage/100) × Total.
Angle = (Value/Total) × 360°.

subtopics.title: Advanced DI – Stacked Bar & Radar Charts

blocks.title: Stacked Bar Chart
blocks.content: Shows the total contribution of multiple categories across different groups. Excellent for comparing total sales with breakdowns.

blocks.title: Radar Chart
blocks.content: A 2D chart used to display multivariate data. Useful for comparing multiple variables (skills, performance).

blocks.title: Example: Stacked Bar Interpretation
given: Stacked Bar chart showing Q1: A=20, B=15, C=10.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Stacked bars combine multiple datasets into one bar segment.
Radar charts are excellent for skill/attribute comparisons.
Both are frequently asked in advanced DI sections.

blocks.title: Shortcut 1 – Assumed Mean Method
blocks.content: For large datasets, assume a mean (A). Mean = A + [ Σ (xᵢ – A) / n ].

blocks.title: Shortcut 2 – Pie Chart Angles
blocks.content: If a sector represents 25%, its angle is 90° (since 25% of 360 = 90). Know common fractions: 50%=180°, 33.33%=120°, 20%=72°.

blocks.title: Shortcut 3 – Average Speed (DI Context)
blocks.content: If DI asks for average of two groups, use (n₁A₁ + n₂A₂)/(n₁+n₂).

blocks.title: Memory Trick
blocks.memory_trick: Mean, Average, Deviation – the three pillars of stats.

blocks.title: Shortcut Summary
points: Use assumed mean for grouped data.
Memorize common percentage-to-angle conversions for pie charts.
Use weighted average formulas for combined groups.

blocks.title: Exam Strategy
blocks.content: Identify the type: Mean/Median/Mode, Dispersion, Table/Bar/Pie/Line, or Mixed DI. Apply the correct formula or shortcut.

blocks.question: In a pie chart, a category with 25% share has a central angle of:
A bar graph shows sales: 2010=100, 2011=120, 2012=144. What is the growth rate from 2010 to 2012?
A table shows marks: 0-10: f=2, 10-20: f=3, 20-30: f=5. Find the mean using midpoints 5, 15, 25.

blocks.title: Post-Test Review
blocks.content: Focus on median/mode formulas, pie chart angles, and table-based DI. These carry the highest weightage in JKSSB.

blocks.title: Final Revision – Statistics & DI
points: Mean = Σx/n or Σfx/Σf.
Median = middle value; Mode = most frequent.
Range = Max-Min; Variance = Σ(x-μ)²/n.
Tables: read columns/rows carefully.
Pie: Angle = (%)×360.
Stacked/Radar: use `datasets` array.
Always practice with PYQs.
